ReplicaSets monitor the cluster and ensure the required number of pods are running.

If a pod fails or is deleted, the ReplicaSet automatically creates a replacement.

flowchart TD ReplicaSet["ReplicaSet"] Pod1["Pod"] Pod2["Pod"] Pod3["Pod"] ReplicaSet --> Pod1 ReplicaSet --> Pod2 ReplicaSet --> Pod3

If one pod fails:

The ReplicaSet restores the desired number of replicas.

docs.title: ReplicaSets and Deployments
docs.text:
In modern Kubernetes usage, ReplicaSets are rarely created directly.

Instead, they are managed automatically by Deployments.

flowchart TD ReplicaSet["ReplicaSet"] Pod1["Pod"] Pod2["Pod"] NewPod["Replacement Pod"] ReplicaSet --> Pod1 ReplicaSet --> Pod2 ReplicaSet --> NewPod

This allows Kubernetes to manage application updates safely.

Key Takeaway

ReplicaSets provide self-healing and scaling capabilities, but they are typically controlled by Deployments.

docs.title: StatefulSets
docs.text:
A StatefulSet is a workload resource designed for applications that require stable identity and persistent storage.

Examples include:

  • databases
  • distributed systems
  • message queues

docs.title: Why StatefulSets Exist
docs.text:
Standard deployments treat pods as interchangeable.

Stateful applications require:

  • stable network identities
  • ordered deployment
  • persistent storage

StatefulSets provide these guarantees.

docs.title: StatefulSet Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TD StatefulSet["StatefulSet"] Pod0["Poddatabase-0"] Pod1["Poddatabase-1"] Pod2["Poddatabase-2"] StatefulSet --> Pod0 StatefulSet --> Pod1 StatefulSet --> Pod2

Each pod receives:

  • a unique name
  • stable storage
  • persistent network identity

Example pod names:

  • database-0
  • database-1
  • database-2

Ordered Deployment

StatefulSets create pods in sequence.

flowchart LR Pod0["database-0"] --> Pod1["database-1"] --> Pod2["database-2"]

If a pod fails, Kubernetes replaces it with the same identity.

docs.title: Persistent Storage
docs.text: StatefulSets are typically used with Persistent Volume Claims so that data survives pod restarts.

docs.title: Key Takeaway
docs.text: StatefulSets enable Kubernetes to run stateful distributed applications safely.

docs.title: Kubernetes How-To Guides
docs.text:
How-to guides provide step-by-step instructions for performing specific tasks in Kubernetes.

Use these guides when you already understand the basics and need to accomplish something quickly.

docs.title: Deployments
docs.text:
Manage and operate application deployments.

  • Rollback Deployment
  • Restart Deployment
  • Update Container Image

docs.title: Networking
docs.text:
Expose and access applications running in the cluster.

  • Expose Service
  • Expose Service with Ingress
  • Port Forward Pod

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Configure applications using Kubernetes resources.

  • Use ConfigMaps
  • Use Secrets
  • Add Environment Variables

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
Work with persistent storage for stateful applications.

  • Mount Persistent Volume
  • Resize Persistent Volume
  • Debug PVC Pending
  • Backup Persistent Volume
  • Migrate Persistent Volume

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Diagnose and fix common Kubernetes issues.

  • Debug a Crashing Pod
  • Kubernetes Error Decoder

docs.title: Application Configuration
docs.text:
Applications running in Kubernetes often require configuration such as environment variables, application settings, and credentials.

Kubernetes provides several mechanisms for managing configuration:

  • Environment variables
  • ConfigMaps for application configuration
  • Secrets for sensitive data

This section explains how to manage configuration for Kubernetes workloads.

docs.title: Common Tasks
docs.text: Task Guide Provide configuration values to applications Use ConfigMaps Store sensitive information Use Secrets Add environment variables to containers Add Environment Variables

docs.title: Key Commands
docs.text:
kubectl get configmaps
kubectl get secrets
kubectl describe configmap <name>
kubectl describe secret <name>
These commands help inspect configuration resources used by applications.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Applications frequently rely on environment variables for runtime configuration.

These values must be injected into containers at runtime.

docs.title: Step 1 — Define Environment Variables
docs.text:
Update container configuration.

deployment.yaml 

env:
  - name: APP_ENV
    value: production
  - name: LOG_LEVEL
    value: info

Full example: 

containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    env:
      - name: APP_ENV
        value: production
      - name: LOG_LEVEL
        value: info

docs.title: Step 2 — Apply Deployment
docs.text:
Run: 

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify Variables
docs.text:
Enter container. 

kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/sh

Check: 

env

Expected: 

APP_ENV=production
LOG_LEVEL=info

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Applications often require configuration such as:

  • API endpoints
  • feature flags
  • application settings

Hardcoding these values inside container images makes them difficult to change.

Kubernetes ConfigMaps allow configuration to be injected into pods.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create a ConfigMap
docs.text:
Create configmap.yaml. 

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  APP_ENV: production
  API_URL: https://api.example.com

Apply it. 

kubectl apply -f configmap.yaml

Verify. 

kubectl get configmap

Expected output: 

NAME         DATA
app-config   2

docs.title: Step 2 — Use ConfigMap in Deployment
docs.text:
Update the deployment.

deployment.yaml 

envFrom:
  - configMapRef:
      name: app-config

Full container example: 

containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    envFrom:
      - configMapRef:
          name: app-config

Apply. 

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify Environment Variables
docs.text:
Enter the pod. 

kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/sh

Check variables. 

env

Expected: 

APP_ENV=production
API_URL=https://api.example.com

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
ConfigMap Not Found

Check: 

kubectl get configmap

Pod Not Restarting

ConfigMap updates do not restart pods automatically.

Restart deployment. 

kubectl rollout restart deployment <deployment-name>

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Applications often require sensitive data:

  • database passwords
  • API keys
  • tokens

These should not be stored in plain configuration.

Kubernetes Secrets store sensitive values securely.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create a Secret
docs.text:
Run: 

kubectl create secret generic db-secret \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD=mysecretpassword

Verify: 

kubectl get secrets

Expected output: 

NAME        TYPE
db-secret   Opaque

docs.title: Step 2 — Use Secret in Deployment
docs.text:
Example container configuration. 

env:
  - name: DB_PASSWORD
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:
        name: db-secret
        key: DB_PASSWORD

Apply deployment. 

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify Secret Injection
docs.text:
Enter the running pod. 

kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/sh

Once inside the container, check that the secret value is available as an environment variable. 

echo $DB_PASSWORD

Example output: 

super-secret-password

If the value appears, the Kubernetes Secret has been successfully injected into the container environment.

docs.title: What Happened
docs.text:
In this guide you:

  • Created a Kubernetes Secret
  • Referenced the secret inside a Deployment
  • Injected the secret as an environment variable
  • Verified the value from inside the container

Secrets allow sensitive data such as:

  • passwords
  • API tokens
  • database credentials

to be stored separately from application code.

docs.title: Clean Up (Optional)
docs.text:
Remove the example resources.

kubectl delete deployment secret-demo
kubectl delete secret db-secret

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you understand Kubernetes Secrets, you can explore:

  • Mounting secrets as files instead of environment variables
  • Using external secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault)
  • Encrypting secrets at rest with Kubernetes encryption providers

Secrets are a critical building block for secure production workloads.

docs.title: Managing Deployments
docs.text:
Deployments manage the lifecycle of applications running in Kubernetes.

They control:

  • the number of running pods
  • application updates
  • rollouts and rollbacks
  • scaling workloads

This section explains how to perform common operational tasks when managing deployments.

docs.title: Common Tasks
docs.text: Task Guide Increase or decrease application replicas Scale a Deployment Restore a previous working version Roll Back a Deployment Restart application pods Restart a Deployment

docs.title: Key Commands
docs.text:
kubectl get deployments
kubectl describe deployment <deployment-name>
kubectl scale deployment <deployment-name> --replicas=5
kubectl rollout status deployment <deployment-name>

These commands allow operators to inspect and manage application deployments.

docs.title: When to Use This Guide
docs.text:
Use this guide when you need to restart all pods in a deployment without changing the deployment configuration.

This is useful when:

  • configuration changes were applied
  • pods become unstable
  • applications require a clean restart

Quick Command

kubectl rollout restart deployment <deployment-name>

docs.title: Step 1 — Restart the Deployment
docs.text:
Restart all pods managed by the deployment.

kubectl rollout restart deployment web-app
Example output:

deployment.apps/web-app restarted

Kubernetes gradually terminates old pods and creates new ones.

docs.title: Step 2 — Monitor the Rollout
docs.text:
Track restart progress. 

kubectl rollout status deployment web-app

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify New Pods
docs.text:
Confirm new pods are running. 

kubectl get pods

New pods will have different identifiers.

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Pods Fail After Restart

If pods fail after restart, inspect logs. 

kubectl logs <pod-name>

Check pod events. 

kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

docs.title: Quick Restart Workflow
docs.text:
kubectl rollout restart deployment web-app
kubectl rollout status deployment web-app
kubectl get pods

docs.title: Related Guides
docs.text:
Deployment Management

  • Scale a Deployment
  • Roll Back a Deployment
  • Update Container Image

Troubleshooting

  • Debug a Crashing Pod

docs.title: When to Use This Guide
docs.text:
Use this guide when a new deployment version introduces errors and you need to quickly restore a previously working version.

Rolling back deployments is common when:

  • a new application release causes failures
  • a container image update breaks functionality
  • configuration changes introduce errors

Quick Commands

kubectl rollout history deployment <deployment-name>
kubectl rollout undo deployment <deployment-name>
kubectl rollout status deployment <deployment-name>

docs.title: Common Rollback Tasks
docs.text: Task Command View rollout history kubectl rollout history deployment web-app Roll back to previous version kubectl rollout undo deployment web-app Roll back to specific revision kubectl rollout undo deployment web-app --to-revision=2 Check rollout progress kubectl rollout status deployment web-app

docs.title: Step 1 — View Deployment History
docs.text:
Check previous deployment versions.

kubectl rollout history deployment web-app

Example: 

REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
1         Initial deployment
2         Update container image
3         Updated configuration

docs.title: Step 2 — Roll Back the Deployment
docs.text:
Restore the previous working revision. 

kubectl rollout undo deployment web-app

Kubernetes replaces the current pods with the previous version.

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify Rollback Status
docs.text:
Check rollout progress. 

kubectl rollout status deployment web-app

Then confirm pods are running. 

kubectl get pods

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Rollback Does Not Fix the Problem

Possible causes:

  • configuration issue still present
  • external dependency failure
  • database migration mismatch

Inspect logs: 

kubectl logs <pod-name>

Quick Rollback Workflow 

kubectl rollout history deployment web-app
kubectl rollout undo deployment web-app
kubectl rollout status deployment web-app

docs.title: Related Guides
docs.text:
Deployment Management - Scale a Deployment - Restart a Deployment - Update Container Image

Troubleshooting

  • Debug a Crashing Pod

docs.title: When to Use This Guide
docs.text:
Use this guide when you need to increase or decrease the number of running pods for an application.

Scaling deployments is commonly required when:

  • application traffic increases
  • testing high availability
  • reducing resource usage
  • preparing for load testing

Quick Commands

kubectl scale deployment <deployment-name> --replicas=5
kubectl get deployment <deployment-name>
kubectl get pods

These commands allow you to quickly adjust the number of pods running in a deployment.

docs.title: Common Scaling Tasks
docs.text: Task Command Increase replicas kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=5 Reduce replicas kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=2 Check replica status kubectl get deployment web-app View running pods kubectl get pods

docs.title: Step 1 — Check Current Deployment Status
docs.text:
List deployments in the namespace.

kubectl get deployments

Example: 

NAME      READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
web-app   2/2     2            2           10m

This indicates the deployment currently runs 2 pods.

docs.title: Step 2 — Scale the Deployment
docs.text:
Increase the number of replicas. 

kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=5

Output: 

deployment.apps/web-app scaled

Kubernetes immediately begins creating additional pods.

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify the New Pods
docs.text:
Check that new pods are starting. 

kubectl get pods

Example: 

web-app-7f8d9c6d4b-abc12
web-app-7f8d9c6d4b-def34
web-app-7f8d9c6d4b-ghi56
web-app-7f8d9c6d4b-jkl78
web-app-7f8d9c6d4b-mno90

docs.title: Step 4 — Confirm Deployment Status
docs.text:
Verify that the deployment has reached the desired state. 

kubectl get deployment web-app

Example: 

NAME      READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE
web-app   5/5     5            5

docs.title: Automatic Scaling with HPA
docs.text:
For dynamic workloads, Kubernetes can automatically scale deployments using the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA).

Create an autoscaler: 

kubectl autoscale deployment web-app --cpu-percent=70 --min=2 --max=10

This tells Kubernetes to:

  • maintain CPU usage around 70%
  • run 2–10 pods depending on load

docs.title: Verify Autoscaling
docs.text:
Check autoscaler status: 

kubectl get hpa

Example: 

NAME      REFERENCE            TARGETS   MINPODS   MAXPODS
web-app   Deployment/web-app   40%/70%   2         10

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
New Pods Remain Pending

Possible causes:

  • insufficient cluster resources
  • node scheduling constraints
  • missing persistent volumes

Investigate using: 

kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

Pods Crash After Scaling

This usually indicates an application issue rather than a scaling problem.

Check logs: 

kubectl logs <pod-name>

Quick Scaling Workflow

Most scaling tasks follow this pattern: 

kubectl get deployments
kubectl scale deployment <deployment-name> --replicas=5
kubectl get pods

docs.title: Related Guides
docs.text:
Deployment Management

  • Roll Back a Deployment
  • Restart a Deployment

Troubleshooting

  • Debug a Crashing Pod
  • View Container Logs

docs.title: When to Use This Guide
docs.text:
Use this guide when you need to deploy a new version of an application by updating the container image used by a deployment.

This commonly happens when:

  • releasing a new application version
  • patching vulnerabilities
  • deploying bug fixes

Quick Command

kubectl set image deployment <deployment-name> <container>=<image>:<tag>

docs.title: Step 1 — Check Current Image
docs.text:
Inspect the deployment configuration.

kubectl describe deployment web-app

Look for: 

Image: web-app:v1

docs.title: Step 2 — Update the Image
docs.text:
Deploy a new container version. 

kubectl set image deployment web-app web-app=web-app:v2

Example output: 

deployment.apps/web-app image updated

docs.title: Step 3 — Monitor the Rollout
docs.text:
Watch the deployment update. 

kubectl rollout status deployment web-app

docs.title: Step 4 — Verify Pods
docs.text:
Check that new pods are running. 

kubectl get pods

Pods should now use the updated image.

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Pods Fail After Image Update

If pods crash after the update, check logs. 

kubectl logs <pod-name>

If necessary, roll back the deployment. 

kubectl rollout undo deployment web-app

Quick Update Workflow 

kubectl set image deployment web-app web-app=web-app:v2
kubectl rollout status deployment web-app
kubectl get pods

docs.title: Related Guides
docs.text:
Deployment Management

  • Scale a Deployment
  • Restart a Deployment
  • Roll Back a Deployment

Troubleshooting

  • Debug a Crashing Pod

docs.title: Kubernetes Networking
docs.text:
Kubernetes networking enables communication between pods, services, and external clients.

Applications in a cluster typically communicate through:

  • Pods for individual containers
  • Services for stable networking endpoints
  • Ingress for external HTTP and HTTPS access

This section explains how to configure and troubleshoot networking for Kubernetes applications.

docs.title: Common Tasks
docs.text: Task Guide Expose an application within the cluster Expose a Service Expose an application to external users Expose a Service with Ingress Access a pod locally for debugging Port-Forward to a Pod

docs.title: Key Commands
docs.text:
kubectl get services
kubectl describe service <service-name>
kubectl port-forward pod/<pod-name> 8080:80

These commands help inspect and interact with cluster networking resources.

docs.title: When to Use This Guide
docs.text:
Use this guide when you want to expose a Kubernetes service to external users through HTTP or HTTPS.

Ingress allows multiple services to share a single external IP address and supports routing based on hostnames or paths.

Quick Workflow

kubectl apply -f ingress.yaml
kubectl get ingress

docs.title: Step 1 — Verify the Service
docs.text:
Ensure your application service exists.

kubectl get services

Example: 

web-app   ClusterIP   10.96.120.45   80/TCP

docs.title: Step 2 — Create an Ingress Resource
docs.text:
Example configuration: 

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: web-app-ingress
spec:
  rules:
  - host: web-app.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: web-app
            port:
              number: 80

Apply the configuration. 

kubectl apply -f ingress.yaml

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify Ingress
docs.text:
Check the ingress resource. 

kubectl get ingress

Example: 

NAME              HOSTS               ADDRESS
web-app-ingress   web-app.example.com 192.168.1.20

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Ingress Has No Address

Your cluster may not have an Ingress controller installed.

Check: 

kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx

Service Returns 404

Verify service name and port in the ingress configuration.

docs.title: Related Guides
docs.text:
• Expose a Service
  • Port Forward Pod

docs.title: When to Use This Guide
docs.text:
Use this guide when you need to make an application accessible through a Kubernetes Service.

Services provide a stable network endpoint that allows other pods or external clients to access your application.

Quick Commands

kubectl expose deployment web-app --port=80 --target-port=8080
kubectl get services

docs.title: Service Types
docs.text: Type Description Use Case ClusterIP Internal cluster access Communication between pods NodePort External access via node IP Simple external exposure LoadBalancer External load balancer Cloud environments

docs.title: Step 1 — Verify the Deployment
docs.text:
Check that your application pods are running.

kubectl get pods

Example: 

web-app-6f9c8c7b7d-xk92
web-app-6f9c8c7b7d-lm12a

docs.title: Step 2 — Create the Service
docs.text:
Expose the deployment. 

kubectl expose deployment web-app \
  --type=ClusterIP \
  --port=80 \
  --target-port=8080

This creates a service that forwards traffic to the application pods.

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify the Service
docs.text:
Check service status. 

kubectl get services

Example: 

NAME      TYPE        CLUSTER-IP     PORT(S)
web-app   ClusterIP   10.96.120.45   80/TCP

docs.title: Step 4 — Test Internal Access
docs.text:
Test the service from another pod. 

kubectl run curl --image=curlimages/curl -it --rm -- sh

Then inside the pod: 

curl http://web-app

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Service Cannot Reach Pods Check service selectors. 

kubectl describe service web-app

Verify labels match pod labels.

Pods Not Ready

Services only route traffic to ready pods.

Check readiness: 

kubectl get pods

Quick Workflow 

kubectl get pods
kubectl expose deployment web-app --port=80 --target-port=8080
kubectl get services

docs.title: Related Guides
docs.text:
Networking

  • Expose Service with Ingress
  • Port Forward Pod

Troubleshooting

  • Debug a Crashing Pod

docs.title: When to Use This Guide
docs.text:
Use this guide when you want to access an application running inside a pod from your local machine.

Port forwarding is useful for:

  • debugging services
  • testing APIs
  • accessing internal dashboards

Quick Command

kubectl port-forward pod/<pod-name> 8080:80

docs.title: Step 1 — Find the Pod
docs.text:
List running pods.

kubectl get pods

Example: 

web-app-6f9c8c7b7d-xk92

docs.title: Step 2 — Forward the Port
docs.text:
Forward a local port to the pod. 

kubectl port-forward pod/web-app-6f9c8c7b7d-xk92 8080:80

Now access the application locally: 

http://localhost:8080

docs.title: Step 3 — Test the Application
docs.text:
Open a browser or run: 

curl http://localhost:8080

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Port Already in Use

Choose a different local port. 

kubectl port-forward pod/web-app 9090:80

Pod Not Running

Verify pod status. 

kubectl get pods

docs.title: Related Guides
docs.text:
Networking

  • Expose a Service
  • Expose a Service with Ingress

docs.title: Storage Overview
docs.text:
docs/kubernetes/how-to/storage/index.md

# Persistent Storage

Containers are ephemeral by default, meaning data stored inside a container is lost when the container stops.

Kubernetes provides persistent storage mechanisms that allow applications to store data outside the container lifecycle.

These mechanisms include:

- **Persistent Volumes (PV)**
- **Persistent Volume Claims (PVC)**
- **Storage Classes**

This section explains how to manage persistent storage for applications running in Kubernetes.

---

## Common Tasks

| Task | Guide |
|-----|------|
| Attach persistent storage to a pod | Mount a Persistent Volume |
| Inspect persistent storage resources | Inspect Persistent Volumes |

---

## Key Commands

```bash
kubectl get pv
kubectl get pvc
kubectl describe pvc <pvc-name>
These commands allow you to inspect and troubleshoot storage resources.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Persistent volumes store critical application data.

Without backups, data loss may occur during:

  • cluster failure
  • accidental deletion
  • storage corruption

docs.title: Step 1 — Identify the PVC
docs.text:
List PVCs. 

kubectl get pvc

Example: 

NAME           STATUS   VOLUME
app-storage    Bound    pvc-1234

docs.title: Step 2 — Launch Temporary Backup Pod
docs.text:
Create backup-pod.yaml. 

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: backup-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: backup
      image: busybox
      command: ["sleep","3600"]
      volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /data
          name: storage
  volumes:
    - name: storage
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: app-storage

Apply: 

kubectl apply -f backup-pod.yaml

docs.title: Step 3 — Create Backup Archive
docs.text:
Enter the pod. 

kubectl exec -it backup-pod -- sh

Create backup: 

tar czf /tmp/backup.tar.gz /data

docs.title: Step 4 — Copy Backup Locally
docs.text:
Run: 

kubectl cp backup-pod:/tmp/backup.tar.gz ./backup.tar.gz

Verify: 

ls -lh backup.tar.gz

Cleanup

Delete backup pod. 

kubectl delete pod backup-pod

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
A PersistentVolumeClaim remains stuck in Pending.

Example: 

kubectl get pvc

Output: 

NAME           STATUS    VOLUME
app-storage    Pending

This means Kubernetes cannot find storage to satisfy the claim.

docs.title: Step 1 — Check Storage Classes
docs.text:
Run: 

kubectl get storageclass

Example: 

NAME                 PROVISIONER
standard (default)   kubernetes.io/aws-ebs

docs.title: Step 2 — Inspect the PVC
docs.text:
Run: 

kubectl describe pvc app-storage

Look at events.

Example error: 

no persistent volumes available

or 

storageclass not found

docs.title: Step 3 — Check Persistent Volumes
docs.text:
Run: 

kubectl get pv

Example: 

NAME      CAPACITY   ACCESS MODES
pv-data   5Gi        RWO

Ensure:

  • size is sufficient
  • access mode matches PVC

docs.title: Step 4 — Fix StorageClass
docs.text:
Add a storage class if missing.

Example PVC: 

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: app-storage
spec:
  storageClassName: standard
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi

Apply again. 

kubectl apply -f pvc.yaml

Expected Result

PVC becomes Bound. 

NAME           STATUS   VOLUME
app-storage    Bound    pvc-1234

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
You may need to migrate data when:

  • changing storage classes
  • moving clusters
  • replacing disks

This requires copying data between volumes.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create New PVC
docs.text:
Create new-pvc.yaml. 

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: new-storage
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi

Apply: 

kubectl apply -f new-pvc.yaml

docs.title: Step 2 — Create Migration Pod
docs.text:
Create migration-pod.yaml. 

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: migration
spec:
  containers:
    - name: migrate
      image: busybox
      command: ["sleep","3600"]
      volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /old
          name: old-volume
        - mountPath: /new
          name: new-volume
  volumes:
    - name: old-volume
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: app-storage
    - name: new-volume
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: new-storage

Apply: 

kubectl apply -f migration-pod.yaml

docs.title: Step 3 — Copy Data
docs.text:
Enter pod. 

kubectl exec -it migration -- sh

Copy data. 

cp -r /old/* /new/

Verify: 

ls /new

docs.title: Step 4 — Update Deployment
docs.text:
Update your deployment to use the new PVC.

Example: 

persistentVolumeClaim:
  claimName: new-storage

Apply the deployment update.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Your application needs persistent storage, but Kubernetes pods are ephemeral.

When a pod restarts:

  • local container data is lost
  • application state disappears
  • logs or uploaded files vanish

You need a Persistent Volume (PV) mounted to your pod.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure:

  • A Kubernetes cluster is running
  • kubectl is configured
  • A StorageClass exists in the cluster

Check available storage classes: 

kubectl get storageclass
Example output: 
NAME                 PROVISIONER
standard (default)   kubernetes.io/gce-pd
fast-ssd             kubernetes.io/aws-ebs

docs.title: Quick Diagnosis (When Storage Is Not Working)
docs.text:
Use this quick checklist.

Check Command PVC status kubectl get pvc PV binding kubectl get pv Pod mount errors kubectl describe pod Volume events kubectl describe pvc

If the PVC is stuck in Pending, a storage class may be missing.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create a PersistentVolumeClaim
docs.text:
Most clusters use dynamic provisioning, so you create a PVC, not a PV.

Create pvc.yaml. 

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: app-storage
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi

Apply it: 

kubectl apply -f pvc.yaml

Verify it: 

kubectl get pvc

Expected output: 

NAME          STATUS   VOLUME      CAPACITY
app-storage   Bound    pvc-12345   5Gi

docs.title: Step 2 — Mount the Volume in a Pod
docs.text:
Update your pod or deployment.

Example deployment.yaml. 

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: storage-demo
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: storage-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: storage-demo
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: app
          image: nginx
          volumeMounts:
            - name: app-storage
              mountPath: /data
      volumes:
        - name: app-storage
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: app-storage

Apply it: 

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify the Volume Mount
docs.text:
Check the pod. 

kubectl get pods

Enter the container. 

kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/sh

Test the storage. 

echo "hello" > /data/test.txt
cat /data/test.txt

If the pod restarts, the file should still exist.

docs.title: Expected Result
docs.text:
Your application now has persistent storage.

Key characteristics:

  • data survives pod restarts
  • volume managed by Kubernetes
  • storage dynamically provisioned

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
PVC Stuck in Pending

Check storage class. 

kubectl get storageclass

Specify it explicitly if required. 

storageClassName: standard

Pod Fails to Start

Check events. 

kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

Look for: 

MountVolume.SetUp failed

Common causes:

  • PVC not bound
  • incorrect access mode
  • storage limit exceeded

Volume Mounted but Not Writable

Check permissions inside the container. 

ls -la /data

Fix with a security context. 

securityContext:
  fsGroup: 1000

docs.title: Pro Tip
docs.text:
Use PVCs with Deployments, not raw pods.

Pods get replaced frequently, but Deployments maintain stable storage attachments.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Your application storage is full and needs to be expanded.

Pods may start failing with errors like: 

no space left on device
or 
disk full

Kubernetes allows PersistentVolumeClaims (PVCs) to be resized if the StorageClass supports expansion.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Check if your storage class allows resizing.

Run: 

kubectl get storageclass

Then inspect the storage class: 

kubectl describe storageclass <storage-class-name>

Look for: 

AllowVolumeExpansion: true

If this is false, resizing will not work.

docs.title: Step 1 — Check Current PVC Size
docs.text:
Run: 

kubectl get pvc

Example output: 

NAME           STATUS   VOLUME       CAPACITY
app-storage    Bound    pvc-12345    5Gi

docs.title: Step 2 — Edit the PVC
docs.text:
Run: 

kubectl edit pvc app-storage

Find: 

resources:
  requests:
    storage: 5Gi

Increase the size: 

resources:
  requests:
    storage: 10Gi

Save and exit.

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify the Resize
docs.text:
Check PVC status. 

kubectl get pvc

Example: 

NAME           STATUS   VOLUME       CAPACITY
app-storage    Bound    pvc-12345    10Gi

docs.title: Step 4 — Verify Inside the Pod
docs.text:
Enter the container. 

kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/sh

Check disk size. 

df -h

Expected output: 

Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use%
/data           10G   2G   8G   20%

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Resize Not Applied

Check events. 

kubectl describe pvc app-storage

Look for: 

Waiting for file system resize

Restart the pod if necessary.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This section provides reference documentation for commonly used Kubernetes commands and objects.

Reference documentation is designed for quick lookup rather than step-by-step learning.

docs.title: kubectl Commands
docs.text:
The following commands are commonly used to interact with Kubernetes clusters.

  • kubectl overview
  • kubectl get
  • kubectl describe
  • kubectl logs
  • kubectl exec
  • kubectl apply

These commands allow users to inspect resources, retrieve logs, run commands inside containers, and manage cluster workloads.

docs.title: Kubernetes Objects
docs.text:
Kubernetes resources are represented as objects managed by the Kubernetes API.

Common objects include:

  • Pods
  • Deployments
  • Services
  • ConfigMaps
  • Secrets
  • StatefulSets

Each object page describes its purpose, configuration fields, and typical usage patterns.

docs.title: Reference Topics
docs.text:

    Configure external HTTP and HTTPS access to services inside the cluster.

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Open Reference

  • :material-package-variant: Helm Basics

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Open Reference

  • :material-chart-line: Resource Limits & HPA

    Control container resource usage and automatically scale applications.

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Open Reference

  • :material-shield-lock: RBAC & Network Policies

  • :material-database: StatefulSets

    Deploy stateful workloads that require persistent storage.

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Open Reference

docs.title: Quick Kubernetes Commands
docs.text:
Common commands used when working with Kubernetes:

kubectl get pods
kubectl get deployments
kubectl get services
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>
kubectl logs <pod-name>

docs.title: Helm Basics
docs.text:
Helm is the package manager for Kubernetes.

It allows applications to be installed using charts, which bundle Kubernetes manifests into reusable packages.

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Concept Description Chart Package containing Kubernetes resources Release Running instance of a chart Repository Collection of charts Values Configuration overrides

docs.title: Install Helm
docs.text:
Example installation:

brew install helm

Check version: 

helm version

docs.title: Add a Repository
docs.text:
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami

Update repositories: 

helm repo update

docs.title: Install a Chart
docs.text:
Example: 

helm install my-nginx bitnami/nginx

docs.title: Upgrade a Release
docs.text: helm upgrade my-nginx bitnami/nginx

docs.title: Uninstall a Release
docs.text: helm uninstall my-nginx

docs.title: Values Example
docs.text:
Override chart configuration: 

helm install my-nginx bitnami/nginx \
  --set service.type=NodePort

Or with a values file: 

helm install my-nginx -f values.yaml bitnami/nginx

docs.title: Useful Commands
docs.text:
List installed releases: 

helm list

Show chart information: 

helm show chart <chart>

Inspect values: 

helm show values <chart>

docs.title: Resource Limits & Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA)
docs.text:
Kubernetes allows you to control how much CPU and memory a container can use and automatically scale applications based on resource usage.

This page provides a quick reference for resource limits, requests, and horizontal pod autoscaling (HPA).

docs.title: Resource Requests vs Limits
docs.text:
Example configuration:

resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "128Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "256Mi"
Field Meaning Requests Minimum resources guaranteed to the container Limits Maximum resources the container is allowed to use

CPU Units

Value Meaning 1000m 1 CPU core 500m 0.5 CPU 250m 0.25 CPU

Explanation:

  • 250m CPU = 0.25 CPU core
  • 128Mi = 128 mebibytes of memory

Requests affect pod scheduling, while limits control runtime enforcement.

docs.title: Why Resource Limits Matter
docs.text:
Without limits:

  • Containers may consume excessive CPU or memory
  • Other workloads may starve
  • Nodes may become unstable

Setting limits helps Kubernetes maintain cluster stability and fairness.

docs.title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)
docs.text:
The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on metrics such as CPU usage.

Example: 

apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: my-app-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: my-app
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
This configuration means:

  • Minimum pods: 2
  • Maximum pods: 10
  • Scale when CPU usage exceeds 70%

docs.title: Useful Commands
docs.text:
Check HPA status: 

kubectl get hpa

View detailed information: 

kubectl describe hpa <name>

docs.title: Metrics Requirement
docs.text:
HPA requires the metrics server to be installed.

Check if it is running: 

kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-system
If it is missing, install the Kubernetes metrics server.

docs.title: Common Scaling Strategy
docs.text:
Typical production configuration:

minReplicas: 2
maxReplicas: 10
CPU target: 60–75%

This provides:

  • Baseline redundancy
  • Ability to scale under load
  • Controlled resource consumption

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• Deployments
  • Resource Requests and Limits
  • Kubernetes Metrics Server

docs.title: Ingress
docs.text:
Ingress provides HTTP and HTTPS routing to services inside a Kubernetes cluster.

It allows external traffic to reach internal services based on rules such as hostnames or URL paths.

Ingress requires an Ingress Controller to function.

Common controllers include:

  • NGINX Ingress Controller
  • Traefik
  • HAProxy

docs.title: Basic Ingress Example
docs.text:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
spec:
  rules:
  - host: myapp.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: my-app-service
            port:
              number: 80

This configuration routes traffic from: 

myapp.example.com → my-app-service

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
View ingress resources: 

kubectl get ingress

Describe an ingress: 

kubectl describe ingress <name>

docs.title: TLS Example
docs.text:
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
      - myapp.example.com
    secretName: tls-secret

The TLS secret must contain: 

tls.crt
tls.key

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Concept Description Ingress Routing rules for external traffic Ingress Controller   Component that implements ingress rules Host rules Route based on domain name Path rules Route based on URL path

docs.title: When to Use Ingress
docs.text:
Use ingress when:

  • Exposing HTTP services
  • Routing multiple apps through one IP
  • Terminating TLS

docs.title: RBAC & Network Policies
docs.text:
Kubernetes provides security controls through:

  • Role Based Access Control (RBAC)
  • Network Policies

RBAC controls who can access Kubernetes resources.

docs.title: Core RBAC Objects
docs.text: Object Purpose Role Permissions within a namespace ClusterRole Permissions cluster-wide RoleBinding Assign role to user/service account ClusterRoleBinding Assign cluster role globally

docs.title:
kind: Role
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
metadata:
  namespace: default
  name: pod-reader
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
Role Binding Example
docs.text:
kind: RoleBinding
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
metadata:
  name: read-pods
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: jane
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: pod-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

docs.title: Network Policies
docs.text:
Network policies control traffic between pods.

Without policies, pods can communicate freely.

docs.title: Example Network Policy
docs.text:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-api
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: frontend

This allows traffic: 

frontend pods → api pods

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
View policies: 

kubectl get networkpolicy

Describe policy: 

kubectl describe networkpolicy <name>

docs.title: StatefulSets
docs.text:
StatefulSets manage stateful applications that require stable identities and persistent storage.

They are commonly used for:

  • databases
  • distributed systems
  • clustered applications

docs.title: Key Characteristics
docs.text: Feature Description Stable Pod Names pods keep consistent names Ordered Deployment pods start sequentially Persistent Storage each pod gets its own volume Stable Network Identity predictable DNS names

docs.title: StatefulSet Example
docs.text:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: mysql
spec:
  serviceName: "mysql"
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mysql
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mysql
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: mysql
        image: mysql:8

Pod Naming

Pods are named sequentially: 

mysql-0
mysql-1
mysql-2

docs.title: Persistent Volume Claims
docs.text:
Each pod receives its own storage: 

mysql-data-mysql-0
mysql-data-mysql-1
mysql-data-mysql-2

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
View StatefulSets: 

kubectl get statefulset

Describe: 

kubectl describe statefulset <name>

Delete: 

kubectl delete statefulset <name>

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
kubectl apply creates or updates Kubernetes resources using configuration files.

It compares the configuration defined in a file with the current cluster state and applies the necessary changes.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: kubectl apply -f [file]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Apply a Configuration File 

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

Apply a Directory of Manifests 

kubectl apply -f manifests/

Apply Resources from a URL 

kubectl apply -f https://example.com/deployment.yaml

Example Manifest 

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: nginx
spec:
  replicas: 2

docs.title: Typical Use Cases
docs.text:
• deploying applications
  • updating configurations
  • managing infrastructure as code

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• kubectl get
  • kubectl describe

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
kubectl describe provides detailed information about a specific Kubernetes resource.

It displays configuration, status information, and recent events.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: kubectl describe [resource] [name]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Describe a Pod 

kubectl describe pod nginx-pod

Describe a Node 

kubectl describe node worker-node-1

Describe a Deployment 

kubectl describe deployment nginx

Output Includes

  • container configuration
  • resource limits
  • environment variables
  • event logs
  • scheduling details

Typical Use Cases

  • debugging failing pods
  • viewing event logs
  • inspecting resource configuration

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• kubectl get
  • kubectl logs

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
kubectl exec runs a command inside a running container within a Kubernetes pod.

It is commonly used for debugging applications or performing administrative tasks.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: kubectl exec [pod-name] -- [command]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Run a Command in a Container kubectl exec nginx-pod -- ls

Open a Shell Inside a Container kubectl exec -it nginx-pod -- /bin/bash

Run a Command in a Specific Container kubectl exec nginx-pod -c nginx -- ls

docs.title: Common Flags
docs.text: Flag Description -it Interactive terminal -c Specify container

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• debugging application containers
  • inspecting container file systems
  • running administrative commands

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
kubectl logs

kubectl describe

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
kubectl get lists Kubernetes resources in the cluster.

It is commonly used to inspect the current state of workloads and infrastructure components.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: kubectl get [resource] [flags]

docs.title: Common Resource Types
docs.text: Resource Description pods Running containers deployments Application deployments services Network access points nodes Cluster worker nodes

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
List Pods 

kubectl get pods

List Pods with Additional Details 

kubectl get pods -o wide

List Deployments 

kubectl get deployments

Watch Resources in Real Time 

kubectl get pods -w

Output Example 

NAME        READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
nginx-pod   1/1     Running   0          2m

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• kubectl describe
  • kubectl logs

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
kubectl logs retrieves logs from containers running in a Kubernetes pod.

It is commonly used for debugging applications and monitoring runtime behavior.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: kubectl logs [pod-name]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
View Pod Logs 

kubectl logs nginx-pod

Stream Logs in Real Time 

kubectl logs -f nginx-pod

View Logs for a Specific Container 

kubectl logs nginx-pod -c nginx

View Previous Container Logs 

kubectl logs --previous nginx-pod

docs.title: Common Flags
docs.text: Flag Description -f Follow logs in real time -c Specify container --previous Show logs from previous container instance

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• kubectl get
  • kubectl describe

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
kubectl is the command-line interface used to interact with a Kubernetes cluster. It allows users to deploy applications, inspect resources, view logs, and manage cluster objects.

kubectl communicates with the Kubernetes API server and translates user commands into API requests.

docs.title: Basic Syntax
docs.text:
kubectl [command] [resource] [name] [flags]

Example: kubectl get pods

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text: Command Purpose kubectl get List resources kubectl describe Show detailed resource information kubectl logs View container logs kubectl exec Execute commands inside a container kubectl apply Create or update resources from configuration files

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
kubectl uses a configuration file called kubeconfig to determine:

  • cluster endpoint
  • authentication credentials
  • default namespace

Default location: ~/.kube/config

docs.title: Check kubectl Version
docs.text: kubectl version

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• kubectl get
  • kubectl describe
  • kubectl logs
  • kubectl exec
  • kubectl apply

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
A ConfigMap stores configuration data for applications running in Kubernetes.

ConfigMaps allow you to separate configuration from container images.

Applications can consume ConfigMaps as:

  • environment variables
  • configuration files
  • command-line arguments

docs.title: Example ConfigMap
docs.text:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  APP_ENV: production
  LOG_LEVEL: info

docs.title: Using ConfigMap as Environment Variables
docs.text:
envFrom:
- configMapRef:
    name: app-config

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
Create ConfigMap: 

kubectl apply -f configmap.yaml

View ConfigMaps: 

kubectl get configmaps

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
ConfigMaps are commonly used for:

  • application configuration
  • environment variables
  • feature flags

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
• Secrets
  • Pods

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
A Deployment manages a set of identical pods and ensures that the desired number of replicas are running.

Deployments provide features such as:

  • rolling updates
  • scaling
  • rollback to previous versions

Deployments are commonly used to run stateless applications.

docs.title: Key Fields
docs.text: Field Description spec.replicas Number of desired pods spec.selector Labels used to identify pods spec.template Pod template used to create pods spec.strategy Update strategy

docs.title: Example Deployment
docs.text:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
Create a deployment: 

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

Scale deployment: 

kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=5

Check deployment status: 

kubectl get deployments

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
Deployments are commonly used for:

  • web applications
  • API services
  • stateless workloads

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
• Pods
  • Services
  • Horizontal Pod Autoscaler

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes.

A pod represents one or more containers that share:

  • network namespace
  • storage volumes
  • lifecycle

Containers inside the same pod can communicate using localhost.

Pods are typically created and managed by higher-level controllers such as Deployments.

docs.title: Key Fields
docs.text: Field Description metadata.name Name of the pod spec.containers List of containers running in the pod spec.volumes Storage volumes attached to the pod spec.restartPolicy Container restart behavior

docs.title: Example Pod Manifest
docs.text:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
Create a pod: 

kubectl apply -f pod.yaml

List pods: 

kubectl get pods

View pod details: List pods: 

kubectl describe pod nginx-pod

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
Pods are typically used for:

  • running containers
  • testing workloads
  • debugging container images In production environments, pods are usually managed by controllers such as Deployments.

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
• Deployments
  • Services

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
A Secret stores sensitive data such as credentials, tokens, and API keys.

Secrets allow applications to access confidential data securely without embedding it in container images.

Examples of secret data include:

  • database passwords
  • API tokens
  • TLS certificates

docs.title: Example Secret
docs.text:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-secret
type: Opaque
data:
  password: cGFzc3dvcmQ=

Values must be base64 encoded.

Using Secrets in a Pod 

env:
- name: DB_PASSWORD
  valueFrom:
    secretKeyRef:
      name: db-secret
      key: password

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
Create secret: 

kubectl apply -f secret.yaml

View secrets: 

kubectl get secrets

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
• restrict access using RBAC
  • avoid committing secrets to version control
  • rotate credentials regularly

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
• ConfigMaps
  • Pods

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
A Service exposes a set of pods as a network endpoint.

Services provide a stable IP address and DNS name for accessing pods.

Since pods are ephemeral and may be recreated, services ensure consistent connectivity.

docs.title: Service Types
docs.text: Type Description ClusterIP Internal cluster access NodePort Exposes service on a node port LoadBalancer External load balancer ExternalName Maps to external DNS name

docs.title: Example Service
docs.text:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: web
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
  type: ClusterIP

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
List services: 

kubectl get services

Describe a service: 

kubectl describe service web-service

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
Services are used for:

  • exposing applications inside the cluster
  • providing load balancing
  • enabling service discovery

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
• Pods
  • Deployments

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
A StatefulSet manages stateful applications that require stable network identities and persistent storage.

Unlike Deployments, StatefulSets provide:

  • stable pod names
  • ordered pod creation
  • persistent storage for each pod

StatefulSets are commonly used for databases and distributed systems.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text: Feature Description Stable network identity Pods have predictable names Persistent storage Each pod gets its own volume Ordered deployment Pods start sequentially

docs.title: Example StatefulSet
docs.text:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: database
spec:
  serviceName: database
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: db
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: db
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
View StatefulSets: 

kubectl get statefulsets

Describe StatefulSet: 

kubectl describe statefulset database

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
StatefulSets are typically used for:

  • databases
  • distributed storage systems
  • message queues

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
• Pods
  • Persistent Volumes

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) automatically adjusts the number of running pods in a deployment or replica set based on resource utilization.

HPA helps applications scale dynamically in response to increased demand.

Common scaling metrics include:

  • CPU usage
  • memory usage
  • custom metrics

docs.title: How HPA Works
docs.text:
The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler periodically checks resource usage metrics and adjusts the number of pod replicas.

Typical workflow:

  1. Metrics server collects resource metrics.
  2. HPA evaluates current utilization.
  3. If usage exceeds the defined threshold, Kubernetes increases replicas.
  4. If usage decreases, replicas may be reduced.

docs.title: Example HPA Configuration
docs.text:
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: web-app-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: web-app
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 60

This configuration scales the deployment when average CPU usage exceeds 60%.

docs.title: Create an HPA Using kubectl
docs.text:
Example command:

kubectl autoscale deployment web-app --cpu-percent=60 --min=2 --max=10

docs.title: View Autoscaler Status
docs.text:
Check HPA configuration: 

kubectl get hpa

View detailed status: 

kubectl describe hpa web-app-hpa

Example output: 

NAME        REFERENCE          TARGETS   MINPODS   MAXPODS
web-app-hpa Deployment/web-app 45%/60%   2         10

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler requires a metrics server to collect resource usage metrics.

Without metrics, autoscaling cannot function.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Define resource requests for containers
  • Set realistic scaling thresholds
  • monitor scaling behaviour in production
  • avoid excessive scaling ranges

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
Resource Requests and Limits

Deployments

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Kubernetes allows you to define resource requests and limits for containers to control how much CPU and memory they can use.

Resource management ensures that workloads receive the resources they need while preventing individual containers from consuming excessive cluster resources.

These settings are defined in the container specification of a Pod or Deployment.

docs.title: Resource Requests
docs.text:
A request defines the minimum amount of CPU or memory guaranteed for a container.

The Kubernetes scheduler uses requests to determine which node can run the pod.

Example:

resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "128Mi"
This ensures the container is scheduled on a node with at least the specified resources available.

docs.title: Resource Limits
docs.text:
A limit defines the maximum amount of resources a container can consume.

If a container exceeds its defined limit:

  • CPU usage is throttled
  • Memory usage may cause the container to be terminated

Example: 

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "256Mi"

Example Pod Configuration 

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "250m"
        memory: "128Mi"
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "256Mi"

Units | Resource | Unit Example | Description | |----------|--------------|-------------| | CPU | 500m | 500 millicpu (0.5 CPU core) | | Memory | 128Mi | 128 mebibytes |

docs.title: View Resource Usage
docs.text:
You can inspect resource usage using: 

kubectl top pods

View node resource usage: 

kubectl top nodes

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Always define resource requests and limits
  • Avoid extremely high limits that could destabilize nodes
  • Monitor resource usage using metrics tools
  • Test workloads under expected load

docs.title: Related Resources
docs.text:
• Horizontal Pod Autoscaler
  • Pod scheduling

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This section provides guidance for diagnosing and resolving common problems in Kubernetes clusters.

When applications fail to deploy or behave unexpectedly, Kubernetes provides several tools to inspect cluster state, container logs, and resource configuration.

Common troubleshooting tasks include:

  • identifying failing pods
  • inspecting container logs
  • reviewing cluster events
  • validating configuration files

docs.title: Basic Troubleshooting Workflow
docs.text:
A typical investigation follows these steps:

  1. Identify the failing resource. 

    kubectl get pods

  2. Inspect detailed resource information. 

    kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

  3. Review container logs. 

    kubectl logs <pod-name>

  4. Execute commands inside the container if necessary. 

    kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/bash

docs.title: Common Problem Categories
docs.text:
Pod Issues

  • CrashLoopBackOff
  • ImagePullBackOff
  • Pods stuck in Pending state

Networking Issues

  • Services not reachable
  • DNS failures
  • Network policy restrictions

Debugging Tools

Kubernetes provides several commands for debugging workloads:

  • kubectl describe
  • kubectl logs
  • kubectl exec
  • kubectl get events

Related Documentation

  • kubectl debugging
  • Pod troubleshooting guides

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
A pod repeatedly crashes and Kubernetes places the container in a CrashLoopBackOff state.

Kubernetes continuously attempts to restart the container but delays future restarts after repeated failures.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
Pods display a status similar to:

kubectl get pods

Output: 

NAME        READY   STATUS             RESTARTS
web-app     0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   5

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text:
Typical causes include:

  • application startup failure
  • invalid container command
  • missing environment variables
  • configuration errors
  • application dependencies unavailable

Investigation

Inspect pod details: 

kubectl describe pod web-app

Check container logs: 

kubectl logs web-app

Check previous container logs: 

kubectl logs --previous web-app

Resolution

Steps depend on the root cause.

Examples:

  • fix application startup command
  • correct environment variables
  • update configuration files
  • ensure dependent services are available

After fixing the issue, redeploy the pod:

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

Prevention

Best practices:

  • validate container commands
  • implement health checks
  • monitor application logs
  • test container images before deployment

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
The container image cannot be pulled from the container registry.

Kubernetes retries the pull operation and eventually places the pod in ImagePullBackOff state.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
kubectl get pods

Output: 

NAME        READY   STATUS             RESTARTS
web-app     0/1     ImagePullBackOff   0

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text:
Common causes include:

  • incorrect image name
  • invalid image tag
  • private registry authentication failure
  • network connectivity issues
  • image does not exist in registry

Investigation

Inspect pod events: 

- kubectl describe pod web-app

Look for messages such as: 

Failed to pull image

Verify the image exists in the container registry.

Resolution

Possible fixes:

  • correct the image name
  • update the image tag
  • configure registry credentials
  • verify network connectivity

Update the deployment configuration: 

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

Prevention

Best practices:

  • verify container images before deployment
  • use image tags instead of latest
  • configure registry authentication

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
kubectl provides several commands that help diagnose problems in Kubernetes clusters.

These commands allow users to inspect resource status, view logs, and interact with running containers.

docs.title: Inspect Cluster Resources
docs.text:
List cluster resources: 

kubectl get pods

List nodes: 

kubectl get nodes

List services: 

kubectl get services

Inspect Resource Details

View detailed information: 

kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

This command shows:

  • container configuration
  • events
  • resource usage
  • scheduling information

View Logs

Inspect container logs: 

kubectl logs <pod-name>

Stream logs in real time: 

kubectl logs -f <pod-name>

Run Commands Inside Containers

Open a shell: 

kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/bash

Run a command: 

kubectl exec <pod-name> -- ls
View Cluster Events

Check recent cluster events: 

kubectl get events

Events can reveal scheduling failures, image pull issues, and container crashes.

Related Documentation

  • CrashLoopBackOff
  • ImagePullBackOff
  • Pod scheduling issues

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: A pod remains in the Pending state and is not scheduled on a node.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
kubectl get pods

Output: 

NAME       READY   STATUS    AGE
web-app    0/1     Pending   3m

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text:
Possible causes include:

  • insufficient cluster resources
  • node selector constraints
  • resource limits too high
  • persistent volume unavailable
  • taints preventing scheduling

Investigation

Check pod events: 

kubectl describe pod web-app

Inspect node availability: 

kubectl get nodes

Check resource usage: 

kubectl describe node <node-name>

Resolution

Possible fixes include:

  • adding additional cluster nodes
  • adjusting resource limits
  • removing restrictive node selectors
  • resolving persistent volume issues

Prevention

Best practices:

  • monitor cluster capacity
  • configure resource requests carefully
  • review scheduling constraints

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Applications cannot access a Kubernetes service.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• application cannot connect to service
  • requests time out
  • service returns connection errors

docs.title: Investigation
docs.text:
Verify the service exists:

kubectl get services

Check endpoints: 

kubectl get endpoints

Inspect service configuration: 

kubectl describe service my-service

Verify pods are running: 

kubectl get pods

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text:
Typical causes include:

  • pods not running
  • incorrect service selector
  • network policy blocking traffic
  • incorrect service type

Resolution

Possible solutions:

  • update service selectors
  • ensure pods match labels
  • review network policies
  • verify container ports

Prevention

Best practices:

  • validate service selectors
  • monitor service endpoints
  • use consistent labeling

docs.title: Kubernetes Tutorials
docs.text:
Tutorials guide you through complete Kubernetes workflows from start to finish.

Unlike how-to guides, tutorials are designed for learning. Each tutorial walks through building and operating real applications inside a Kubernetes cluster.

docs.title: Learning Roadmap
docs.text:
Follow the tutorials in this order to build your Kubernetes skills:

Beginner → Intermediate → Advanced

  • Beginner – Learn the core Kubernetes workflow
  • Intermediate – Deploy real applications with configuration, storage, and networking
  • Advanced – Explore production-grade Kubernetes patterns

docs.title: Tutorial Levels
docs.text:
• :material-school: Beginner Tutorials

Start here if you are new to Kubernetes.

Learn the fundamentals of pods, deployments, and services.

:octicons-arrow-right-24: Open Beginner Tutorials

  • :material-progress-check: Intermediate Tutorials

Learn real-world deployment patterns including configuration, secrets, storage, and ingress.

:octicons-arrow-right-24: Open Intermediate Tutorials

  • :material-rocket-launch: Advanced Tutorials

Explore production-grade Kubernetes topics such as autoscaling, security, and progressive deployments.

:octicons-arrow-right-24: Open Advanced Tutorials

docs.title: Recommended Learning Path
docs.text:
If you are new to Kubernetes, follow this order:

  1. Beginner Tutorials
  2. Intermediate Tutorials
  3. Advanced Tutorials

Each section builds on the concepts introduced in the previous level.

docs.text:

````
